tldr: Note: Rekognition is a computer-vision API and DeepL is a translation API — these solve different problems; this guide covers the case where you are decommissioning Rekognition and standing up DeepL for translation, not a like-for-like swap.
why_switch: DeepL targets text translation, not image or video analysis — use it only if your real need is localization
Public freemium pricing vs. AWS per-image / per-minute billing
Lower lock-in profile for translation use cases
Simple REST API and SDKs aimed at translation workflows
If you need image analysis, DeepL is not a replacement — keep a vision provider
what_to_export: Any stored Rekognition labels, detections or face metadata you rely on
Custom Labels models and training datasets
Collections of indexed faces (if used) and their external IDs
S3 references and IAM policies tied to Rekognition
Logs of API usage for cost and audit reconciliation
Source text, glossaries, and translation memories you want to bring to DeepL
Any pipeline configuration calling Rekognition that needs to be retired or rerouted
export_method: For Rekognition, retrieve any indexed face collections, custom-label models and stored detection results via the AWS SDK and S3, then archive them to your own storage before deletion. Capture CloudWatch / billing reports for audit. Consult AWS's official Rekognition documentation for current export and deletion procedures.
import_method: Stand up DeepL for translation workflows by integrating its REST API or official SDK, configuring language pairs and any glossaries, and routing your localization pipeline through it. Keep this work fully separate from any computer-vision pipeline you previously ran on Rekognition. Consult DeepL's official docs for current endpoints, authentication, and SDK versions.

concept_mapping.note: DeepL does not provide image analysis
No equivalent in DeepL — pair with another vision vendor if needed
Out of scope for a translation API
Store securely and rotate per your secrets policy
Forecast based on translated character volume, not images
Only relevant portion that maps cleanly between the two

gotchas: These products solve different problems — confirm you are not losing a needed capability
If image analysis is still required, keep a vision provider in parallel
Translation cost models are character-based; volume forecasting differs from per-image billing
Glossaries and translation memory require curation, not raw text dumps
DeepL language coverage is strong but not exhaustive — verify all target locales
Audit any downstream services still calling Rekognition before turning off IAM access

email.subject: Decommissioning Amazon Rekognition usage
email.body:
Hello AWS team,

We are winding down our use of Amazon Rekognition as part of a stack consolidation.

Please confirm the recommended steps to delete our Rekognition-related resources (custom models, face collections, stored outputs) and to ensure billing stops at the appropriate date.

Thanks,
